title: Hackers can use 9 of the most popular AI tools to assemble massive botnets
gist: Tel Aviv University researchers disclosed 'HalluSquatting,' a scalable pull-based prompt-injection attack that exploits LLM coding agents' tendency to hallucinate repository/skill identifiers, letting attackers pre-register squatted names to seed reverse shells and assemble botnets across nine popular AI coding tools including Cursor, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot, and Windsurf.

insights.claim: A new pull-based prompt-injection attack named HalluSquatting exploits LLM coding agents' tendency to hallucinate resource identifiers, letting attackers pre-register the fabricated names and seed them with reverse-shell instructions to compromise devices at scale without targeting each victim.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Signals a step-change in AI-supply-chain risk that raises security spend and liability exposure for firms deploying autonomous coding agents.
insights.quote: A new attack the researchers have named HalluSquatting has the potential to assemble massive botnets, perform large-scale DDoSes, and infect devices at scale, a first for prompt-injection attacks.

insights.claim: The attack is scalable because it targets popular trending resources, maximizing the likelihood a squatted resource is retrieved, letting one attacker compromise many users with minimal effort.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Scalability is what turns a niche AI bug into systemic botnet/ransomware infrastructure risk worth pricing into cyber-insurance and security budgets.
insights.quote: The scalable property of the attack enables the attacker to compromise a large number of users with minimal effort by targeting popular resources, thereby maximizing the likelihood that the squatted resource will be retrieved

insights.claim: Coding agents routinely use high-privilege command lines to pull and run code from third-party repositories and registries, so attacker-registered resources containing reverse-shell instructions get executed via the agents' integrated shells.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: The privilege level granted to agents is the core vulnerability surface; tools that sandbox terminal access will command a security premium.

insights.claim: Nine popular AI coding assistants and agents are susceptible to HalluSquatting: Cursor, Cursor CLI, Gemini CLI, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot, Cline, OpenClaw, ZeroClaw, and NanoClaw.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Names the exposed vendor set an investor can watch for remediation announcements or reputational fallout.
insights.locator: The attack works against AI coding assistants

insights.claim: The hallucination flaw exists at the foundational level of all six major LLMs tested: Gemini-2.5-flash, Gemini-2.5-pro, GPT-5.1, GPT-5.2, Sonnet-4.5, and Opus-4.5.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: No single vendor can market immunity; the flaw is industry-wide, meaning mitigation is a shared cost across all frontier model providers.
insights.locator: Not only are these hallucinations inevitable

insights.claim: When a coding agent is told to clone a popular new repository, the LLM hallucinates its correct location up to 85 percent of the time, and hallucination can hit 100 percent for trending 'skills' because those resources aren't in the training data.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Extreme hallucination rates on fresh resources quantify how reliably attackers can pre-position squats around any trending package.
insights.locator: LLMs don't know how to say

insights.claim: LLMs correctly resolve repositories published before 2019 with a 0.9 percent mean hallucination rate but fabricate slugs for repositories published in 2025 at a 92.4 percent mean rate.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Recency is the risk axis: the newer and more trending the dependency, the higher the exploitation probability, guiding where defenses must concentrate.
insights.quote: The same LLMs fabricate slugs for repositories published in 2025 with a mean hallucination rate of 92.4 percent.

insights.claim: All six models follow a predictable self-referential hallucination pattern, producing repo-name/repo-name slugs that treat the repository name as the owner, and exploiting it requires no model probing.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Predictable error patterns mean attacks can be automated at low cost, raising the urgency of registry-level defenses.
insights.quote: All six models produce repo-name/repo-name slugs that treat a repository name as the owner. Exploiting the pattern requires no model probing.

insights.claim: Typosquatting precedent from 2016 saw 214 booby-trapped packages on PyPI, RubyGems, and NPM executed over 45,000 times across 17,000+ domains, with more than half granted administrative rights.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Historical base rate shows squatting attacks scale fast and persist, framing HalluSquatting as a durable, recurring threat category.

insights.claim: Compromised agentic machines can be aggregated into botnets for large-scale cryptocurrency mining or DDoS attacks, and used to scale ransomware campaigns across networks for financial gain.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Maps the monetization path attackers will pursue, informing which downstream sectors (crypto, cloud, cyber-defense) face rising threat volume.

insights.claim: Security experts argue agents will inevitably be fooled given the level of agency granted them, so resilience rather than prevention should be the default design assumption.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI-agent security market toward containment/resilience tooling as a durable spend category.
insights.quote: They *are* going to get fooled one way or the other. That should be our assumption, and we should be resilient to that.

insights.claim: AI tool makers overstate efficiency gains while staying quiet about inherent flaws, and HalluSquatting shows users must still manually double-check the location of every resource an agent incorporates.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Undercuts productivity-ROI narratives that support AI-tooling valuations; hidden verification overhead erodes claimed automation gains.
insights.quote: some of the efficiencies are exaggerated since, at the end of the day, users must double-check details such as the location for each resource incorporated into a project.
